summary: Gruber explores reader feedback on how a rumored USB-only MacBook might handle charging without MagSafe. He evaluates three recurring ideas: treating it like an iPad (used unplugged), moving MagSafe elsewhere on the cable or adapter, and inductive charging like the Apple Watch. He finds the iPad comparison flawed since MacBooks serve different use cases, considers a MagSafe-to-USB dongle 'gross,' and doubts inductive technology can deliver enough power for a laptop. He leans toward some form of MagSafe built into the AC adapter as the most likely solution, while also speculating about haptic trackpad feedback.
key_points: The iPad analogy breaks down because MacBooks and iPads serve fundamentally different use cases and ergonomic contexts
Even with extraordinary battery life, people will plug in while using a MacBook, making trip protection still relevant
A shorter iPad-style cable could reduce tripping risk but would frustrate users who leave home with an uncharged laptop
Inductive charging powerful enough for a laptop doesn't exist yet, and placement on the device poses physical design problems
A non-clicking trackpad with haptic feedback would be a significant interaction design change requiring careful execution
money_quotes: The whole point of MacBooks and iPads being separate devices with completely different form factors is that people use them for different tasks, and in different environments.
Even if this new MacBook has absolutely staggering battery life, if people can plug it in while using it, they will, at least sometimes, which means MagSafe trip protection would still be useful.
Requiring a dongle would be gross (and easily lost).
That's a great idea, but I'm not aware of any inductive technology that carries sufficient current to power something as big as a laptop.
If any company could bring such technology to market this year, it'd be Apple, but I'll believe it when I see it.
Apple has always prided itself on the quality of its trackpads — they're the best in the industry, by a long shot.
key_insight: Removing MagSafe from a MacBook isn't as simple as treating it like an iPad — laptops have fundamentally different usage patterns that still demand trip protection in some form.
